title: Large model for fault diagnosis of industrial equipment based on a knowledge graph construction

agents.description: A monolithic agent that integrates DeepSeek-V3 for encoding natural language descriptions of vibration features, combined with graph attention networks for structural reasoning and MobileNetV3 for time-frequency map feature extraction. It performs collaborative fault diagnosis reasoning across text, graph, and time-frequency map modalities.

genai_model.finetuning_dataset_description: The model is fine-tuned on vibration signal data from the ABLT-1A bearing degradation test rig (6205 type rolling bearing) with six fault types: normal, inner race fault, outer race fault, ball fault, inner and outer race compound fault, and outer race and ball compound fault. Fine-tuning freezes the first 24 layers and fine-tunes the last three Transformer blocks.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: The system follows a single-step orchestration pattern where vibration signals are processed through a pipeline: feature extraction (time-frequency features + time-frequency maps), knowledge graph construction and retrieval, text prompt generation fed into DeepSeek-V3, graph encoding via multi-head attention GAT, image feature extraction via MobileNetV3, and finally multimodal fusion via a gated unit for fault classification output.

human_integration.human_integration_description: The system is primarily automated for industrial fault diagnosis. Human involvement is limited to the experimental setup, dataset collection using LabVIEW, and evaluation of results. No explicit human-in-the-loop mechanism during inference is described.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Industrial equipment fault diagnosis, specifically for rolling bearings and gearboxes, using multimodal vibration signal analysis to detect and classify fault types including single and compound faults.
retrieval.problem_description: Industrial equipment fault diagnosis faces challenges of high multimodal data heterogeneity (vibration signals, time-frequency maps), insufficient modeling of fault semantic relationships, and weak adaptability of large language models to industrial non-text modalities. Traditional methods rely on single-modal data and manual feature engineering, while existing deep learning methods lack transparency and struggle with compound fault diagnosis and sparse fault samples.
retrieval.proposed_solution: A multimodal fault diagnosis framework integrating a dynamically constructed time-frequency knowledge graph with the large model DeepSeek-V3. The framework uses unsupervised knowledge graph construction via mutual information (MIC) and dynamic time warping (DTW) to mine semantic relationships from vibration signals. DeepSeek-V3 encodes natural language descriptions of vibration features, while graph attention networks encode subgraph structure and MobileNetV3 extracts time-frequency map features. A gated unit fuses the three modalities, and a graph consistency loss constrains predictions to align with knowledge graph logic.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: Controlled experimental evaluation on a custom bearing fault dataset (ABLT-1A test rig) and the SEU gearbox dataset. The proposed method is compared against eight benchmark models (SVM, XGBoost, 1D-CNN, CNN-FC, CNN-SA, WKNN, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMa-2) using accuracy and F1-score metrics. Ablation studies evaluate contributions of the knowledge graph module, multimodal fusion strategies, and the consistency loss module. Additional robustness tests under varying noise levels (SNR -5 to 25 dB) and few-shot scenarios (5%-40% training data) are conducted.

decision_quality_improvement: Proposed method achieves average accuracy of 95.8% across six fault types, surpassing best benchmark LLaMa-2 by 4.2%.
F1-score of 0.942, representing improvements of 5.3-18.1% over other benchmark methods.
Knowledge graph module improves IORF ACC from 84.3% to 93.7% and ORBF F1-score from 0.812 to 0.901 compared to KG-Loss variant.
Multimodal fusion achieves 93.7% ACC for IORF, 13.9% higher than KG-only variant.
Graph consistency loss improves IORF F1-score from 0.861 to 0.928 and ORBF F1-score from 0.821 to 0.901.
On SEU gearbox dataset, achieved average accuracy of 96.5% and F1-score of 0.958, outperforming LLaMa-2 by 3.7%.
In few-shot scenario with only 5% training data, framework achieves 80.2% average accuracy; ACC increases by 14.5% as training data expands from 5% to 20%.
response_time_impact: Proposed method inference delay is 38 ms, lower than GPT-3.5-Turbo (62 ms) and LLaMa-2 (55 ms), though higher than traditional methods (SVM: 12 ms, XGBoost: 18 ms, 1D-CNN: 25 ms).
Multi-modal fusion increases inference latency compared to single-modal approaches.
reliability_metrics: At SNR of -5 dB (highly noisy conditions), average ACC is 85.3%, demonstrating robustness under strong noise.
Knowledge graph dynamic edge pruning suppresses noise interference by removing low-confidence edges.

ce_technique_effectiveness.knowledge-graph: Significantly improves compound fault diagnosis; IORF ACC improved from 84.3% to 93.7% and ORBF F1-score from 0.812 to 0.901 when knowledge graph is included.
ce_technique_effectiveness.retrieval-augmented-generation: Subgraph retrieval from knowledge graph based on MIC-filtered fault nodes enables structured reasoning and improves semantic alignment between features and fault types.
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Structured text prompts encoding vibration feature values and their physical interpretations enable DeepSeek-V3 to generate meaningful semantic vectors for fault diagnosis.

failure_modes: Normal states slightly misclassified when knowledge graph imposes strong generalization constraints on normal-state features.
Normal state F1-score slightly increases when consistency loss is removed, suggesting possible over-suppression of noise-related features in constrained model.
Image-only model yields lowest ACC for normal states as uniform texture in time-frequency map is easily confused with early-stage faults.
GPT-3.5 suffers from higher false alarm rate due to absence of local time-frequency feature fusion and structured knowledge from graphs.
limitations: Challenges remain in domain generalization and adaptability to extreme operating environments.
Multi-modal fusion increases inference latency compared to single-modal approaches.
Model relies on supervised fine-tuning which may limit generalization with sparse fault samples.
Semantic gap between LM text generation paradigm and industrial multimodal data can lead to ambiguous physical meanings.
Future work needed on cross-modal alignment algorithms for multi-sensor data and adversarial training for highly interfered environments.

identified_research_gaps: Domain generalization and adaptability to extreme operating environments
Cross-modal alignment algorithms for multi-sensor data
Improving model stability in highly interfered environments through adversarial training
Lack of multimodal semantic alignment in existing LM methods
Integration of fault mechanisms and logical constraints within the graph
Real-time optimization capabilities for industrial fault diagnosis
